posture.rationale: Buyer posture remains ACCUMULATE because average network pressure cooled to 36.7, even though tariff/policy still leads at 51.5 and volatility remains watch-tier at 46.4. That favors staged execution and tighter re-checks rather than passively waiting.

contractImpactCalculator.title: Contract Impact Calculator
contractImpactCalculator.body: Current contract math still favors staged execution. Average pressure is 36.7, tariff/policy leads at 51.5, volatility sits at 46.4, and ZL is 74.53; that keeps tranche spacing superior to either one-shot buying or an idle wait posture.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Quantify whether current market structure supports staged, one-shot, or defer-and-watch soybean-oil contracting.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: When one risk channel stays clearly above the network average, spreading execution windows lowers timing regret more effectively than chasing the perfect fill.
quantResearchProtocol: Use average pressure, lead-driver score, and lead-driver spread as the core structure inputs.
Separate staged execution from one-shot execution by comparing latency risk with over-coverage risk.
Keep the recommendation buyer-side and replacement-cost focused.
Define explicit invalidation triggers tied to energy and volatility moderation.
inferenceConstraints: Do not recommend one-shot execution while energy remains the lead channel.
Do not describe staging without quantifying why staging is superior.
Do not present procurement guidance as a trade idea.
outputRequirements: Quote the score math behind the recommendation.
List the conditions that would justify changing the structure.

sourceRecords.recordHint: current risk-factor synthesis used for contract timing
ZL daily close used for current buyer cost framing

notes: Contract timing is tied to cross-driver spread math, not generic caution language.
Buyer recommendation stays replacement-cost focused rather than speculative.

factorWaterfall.title: Factor Waterfall
factorWaterfall.body: Current procurement pressure order is tariff threat (51.5), volatility transmission (46.4), China flow risk (38.6), energy pass-through (26.1), then crush pressure (21.0). The stack matters because the lead channel is watch-grade, not crisis-grade, so posture stays concentrated but not defensive.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Rank the active drivers by marginal buyer impact so monitoring time stays on the right channels.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Procurement risk is shaped by channel ordering and spread, not just by the average level of stress.
quantResearchProtocol: Order each driver by current score and note the spread from the network average.
Separate dominant drivers from background channels.
Call out whether the stack is broad or concentrated.
Translate the stack into concrete monitoring priority.
inferenceConstraints: Do not collapse the waterfall into an undifferentiated summary.
Do not hide conflict or concentration inside the stack.
Do not assign confidence without freshness context.
outputRequirements: State whether the stack is broad or concentrated.
Explain the monitoring consequence for a buyer.

sourceRecords.recordHint: driver ordering and contribution math for current strategy stack

notes: Waterfall ordering is derived from the active driver scores in the refreshed dashboard snapshot.
Dominance language is reserved for channels materially above the network average.

riskMetrics.title: Risk Metrics
riskMetrics.body: Buyer downside asymmetry is still elevated enough to justify discipline, not panic: average pressure is 36.7, policy uncertainty is 439.64, and managed money remains net long 158,102 contracts or 22.45% of open interest. That says latency risk is real, but controlled tranche execution is still safer than rush coverage.

strategicSpecialInstructions.cardTopic: Buyer Risk Math and Tail Exposure
strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Express near-term soybean-oil procurement risk in explicit asymmetry and latency-risk terms.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: For buyers, moderate average pressure can still justify action if one live channel remains strong enough to reprice costs faster than the physical market is moving.
quantResearchProtocol: Compare ZL movement with crude movement and the network average.
Measure the premium of the lead channel over the stack average.
Classify whether timing risk or over-hedge risk is larger.
Tie the answer to cadence and monitoring rules.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call risk neutral when one channel is still materially elevated.
Do not omit latency implications from the math.
outputRequirements: State the risk regime in buyer language.
Explain latency risk versus over-coverage risk.

sourceRecords.recordHint: average network risk and lead-driver spread

notes: Risk framing combines active network pressure with weekly positioning rather than treating either one as sufficient.
Latency risk is expressed in buyer cadence terms, not trader return terms.
